originalTitle: 'Peace is a gradual thing': How land, cattle and identity fuel a deadly Nigerian conflict
title: Making Peace in Nigeria: A Kid's Guide
content:
Hello, super friends! I'm your Kid Reporter from Africa! Today, we're going on an important adventure to a place called Nigeria. Nigeria is a big, wonderful country in Africa, full of amazing people, animals, and beautiful places.

Welcome to Nigeria!

Imagine a country with so many different kinds of people! People who speak different languages and have different traditions. That's Nigeria! It's like having a big, colourful garden with all sorts of flowers. Everyone is special! Did you know? Nigeria is the most populated country in Africa! That means many, many people live there. Now, let's zoom into a special part of Nigeria called Plateau State. Plateau State is a really pretty place. It has lovely hills and green fields. Many people in Plateau State are farmers. They grow yummy food for everyone. Other people keep animals, like cows and goats. These animals help them get milk and food, and sometimes they sell them to earn money.

Living in Plateau State

Imagine you live in a place with green fields and big, tall trees. That's a bit like Plateau State! People live in villages and towns there. They work hard to take care of their families. Farming is super important because it gives us food to eat every day. Keeping cows is also very important. Cows give us fresh milk! They also help people earn a living. Did you know? Cows are herbivores, which means they only eat plants, like grass! Sometimes, even in beautiful places, people can have problems. It's like when you and your friend both want to play with the same toy. In Plateau State, sometimes people don't always get along. This can make people sad or even scared. We call this 'conflict' or 'fighting'.

Why Do People Fight?

It's tricky, but usually, people fight because of a few big things. Imagine if you and your friend want the same cookie. That's a small fight. In Plateau State, the reasons are bigger.

The Importance of Land

One big reason is land. Land is super important for everyone. It's where you build your house. It's where farmers grow their crops, like maize and beans. And it's where the cows graze and find yummy grass to eat. Sometimes, people argue about who owns which piece of land. They might think: "This is my land for farming!" And another person might think: "No, this is where my cows should eat!" When they don't agree, problems can start. Think about your garden or your playground. That's your space, right? For people in Plateau State, land is like their garden and their playground, but it's also their supermarket and their home! It provides food for them and their animals. So, when people argue about land, it's a very big deal. Did you know? Without land for farming, we wouldn't have many of the fruits and vegetables we eat!

Cows and Crops

Another big reason is cows, or 'cattle'. For some people in Plateau State, their cows are like their treasure! They get milk, and they can sell them to earn money for their families. But sometimes, these cows walk onto other people's farms and eat their crops! Imagine if someone's pet ate all your homework! The farmer gets very upset because their food is gone. And the person with the cows might say: "But my cows need to eat!" This can cause big disagreements. It's a tricky situation. The farmers need their crops to grow food. The cow owners need their cows to eat grass to stay healthy and give milk. When these two needs clash, it can lead to sadness and anger. Everyone wants to protect what is important to them. Did you know? A group of cows is called a 'herd'!

Different People, Different Ways

Sometimes, people belong to different groups. They might have different ways of doing things, or speak different languages, or have different beliefs. We call this their 'identity'. It's like some kids love playing football and others love drawing. They are different, but both are good! But sometimes, people don't understand or respect these differences. They might think their way is the only way. This can make it hard for everyone to live together peacefully. Imagine a classroom where everyone thinks differently. Some like blue, some like red. Some like to read, some like to run. It's good to have different interests! In Plateau State, people from different groups have lived side-by-side for a long time. But sometimes, when there are problems about land or cows, these differences can become bigger. People might forget that everyone is a person who wants to be safe and happy. Did you know? Nigeria has over 250 ethnic groups! That means lots of different cultures and traditions!

When Things Aren't Fair (No Justice)

Now, imagine there's a problem, like someone took your toy. What do you do? You tell a grown-up, right? You expect the grown-up to be fair and help you fix it. This is like 'justice'. Justice means everything is fair and problems are fixed in the right way. But sometimes, people in Plateau State feel like the grown-ups (like the police or other helpers) don't always fix things fairly. Maybe someone's land was taken, but no one helped them get it back. Or someone's crops were eaten, but no one made it right. When people feel like things are not fair, they get very, very sad and angry. They might try to fix things by themselves, and that can lead to more fighting. Did you know? A judge's job is to make sure justice is served and things are fair for everyone!

When We Don't Trust Our Helpers

Then there's 'trust'. Trust means you believe someone will help you and keep you safe. Imagine you fall down, and you trust your parent or teacher to help you up. In Plateau State, sometimes people don't trust the police or other helpers to keep them safe from fighting. Maybe they called for help, but help didn't come quickly. Or maybe they feel the helpers only help one group of people. When there is no trust, people feel alone and unprotected. They might try to protect themselves and their families, and this can also make the fighting worse. It's so important to have people we can trust, isn't it? Like when you trust your doctor to make you feel better. In Plateau State, when people don't trust the people who are supposed to protect them, it's very scary. They might feel like they have to solve all problems by themselves. This makes it harder for everyone to live peacefully and safely. Did you know? Trust is like a bridge! It connects people and helps them work together.

Building Peace, Step by Step

The grown-ups say that 'peace is a gradual thing'. What does that mean? It means peace doesn't just happen overnight. It's like growing a plant. You have to water it every day, make sure it gets sunlight, and take care of it slowly, bit by bit. Making peace also takes time, patience, and effort from everyone. To build peace, everyone needs to work together. People need to talk to each other, not fight. They need to listen to each other's feelings and try to understand. The helpers (like the police) need to make sure they are fair to everyone, no matter what group they belong to. They need to show people that they *can* be trusted. When everyone is fair and people trust each other, the fighting can stop. People can go back to farming their land and taking care of their cows without fear. They can live together as good neighbours, like friends in a big, colourful garden. Did you know? Talking about problems calmly is called "dialogue," and it's a super important step to finding solutions!

Let's All Be Peacemakers!

Even as kids, you can be a peacemaker! When you share your toys, when you listen to your friends, when you play nicely with everyone, you are helping to build peace. And when you learn about other people and their ways, you are helping people understand each other better. It's important to remember that everyone in Plateau State, just like you, wants to be safe, happy, and have enough food. By understanding these problems, we can all hope for a future where everyone lives together in peace. Let's send our happy thoughts and wishes for peace to our friends in Nigeria!
summary: People in Nigeria's Plateau State sometimes fight over land and cows because they feel things are not fair and they don't trust helpers, making it hard to live peacefully.
whyMatters: It's important because when people fight, they get hurt and scared, and it stops them from living happily and safely, so everyone needs to work together for fairness and peace.

originalTitle: Leading anti‑racism activist in Tunisia jailed for eight years
title: A Story of Kindness: Saadia from Tunisia
content:
Hello, Super Listeners!

Welcome to our special news report from Africa! Today, we're going to talk about a very kind and brave lady named Saadia Mosbah. She lives in a beautiful country in Africa called Tunisia. Saadia is a bit like a superhero, but instead of wearing a cape, she uses her big heart and strong voice to help people. She believes everyone deserves kindness and a safe place to live, no matter where they come from.

Meet Saadia! A Friend to Everyone

Imagine someone who always wants to make sure everyone feels happy and welcome. That's Saadia Mosbah! She is known as an 'activist.' An activist is a special kind of grown-up who speaks up when they see something that isn't fair or kind. They work hard to make the world a better place for everyone. Saadia’s special mission is to make sure everyone is treated fairly. This is called 'anti-racism.' It means believing that all people are equal and should be treated with love, no matter what their skin color is, what language they speak, or where their family comes from. It's like saying, "Let's all be friends on the playground, no matter what we look like!" Did you know? Africa is a huge continent with many different countries, like Tunisia! It has amazing animals, diverse cultures, and warm, friendly people.

Who Are Migrants?

Sometimes, people have to leave their homes and move to a new place. Maybe their old home isn't safe anymore because of bad weather or other grown-up problems. Or maybe they are looking for a new chance, like a safer place for their children to go to school or a new job for their parents. These people are called 'migrants.' Think about it like this: when you go to a brand new school, or if your family moves to a new town, you might feel a little bit shy or need some help finding your way. Migrants feel the same way! They need extra kindness, smiles, and help to feel welcome and safe in their new home. Saadia Mosbah wanted to make sure these new friends who came to Tunisia felt welcome. She wanted them to have good food, a safe place to sleep, and a chance to be happy and healthy, just like everyone else. She believed it was important to open their arms and hearts to these new neighbours.

Speaking Up for What's Right

Saadia didn't just *think* about being kind; she *did* something about it! She talked to other grown-ups, she wrote letters, and she campaigned. Campaigning means trying to get lots of people to understand a problem and work together to fix it. She spoke up even more when some powerful grown-ups, like the President of Tunisia, Kais Saied, said things that made it harder for migrants. The President and some others worried that too many people were coming, and it might change their country too much. But Saadia believed that helping people who need it most, and showing kindness, was always the most important thing to do. She believed that a country becomes stronger and happier when everyone is kind and welcoming to each other. She stood up for the idea that everyone should be treated fairly, just like we learn to share our toys and be fair in our games.

A Sad Piece of News

Now, here's the part of the story that might make us feel a little sad. Because Saadia spoke up so much, and because some of the grown-ups in charge didn't agree with her helping the migrants, something difficult happened. Saadia Mosbah was put in jail. This means she has to stay in a special building for a very long time – for eight years. It's a very, very long time. This happened because she was trying to help people and speak out for what she believed was right and fair. It shows that sometimes, even when you are trying to do good things, it can be very hard and grown-ups might not always agree. Did you know? Being brave means doing what's right, even when it's scary or hard. Saadia is very brave!

Why Is Kindness So Important?

Saadia's story teaches us something very, very important. It teaches us to always be kind, even when it's difficult. It teaches us to treat everyone like a friend, no matter where they come from or what they look like. Imagine if you were new in a place, and didn't know anyone. Wouldn't you want someone to smile at you, help you find your way, or invite you to play? That's exactly what Saadia was doing for migrants in Tunisia. Kindness is like a special light that makes the world brighter for everyone. When we are kind, we make others feel safe, happy, and loved. And when we stand up for kindness, we help make sure everyone gets treated fairly.

What Can We Learn and Do?

Even though Saadia is in a difficult place right now, her message of kindness and fairness is still very loud and clear. We can all be like Saadia in our own small ways every day! Here are some ideas: * Include everyone in your games at school or in the park. * Share your snacks or toys with a friend. * If you see someone new, give them a friendly wave and a smile. * If you see someone being treated unfairly, tell a trusted grown-up. * Always use kind words. Being kind and speaking up for what is right is a very brave thing to do. You have the power to make a difference too!

Let's Keep Hope Alive!

Saadia's story reminds us to keep hoping for a world where everyone is treated with love and respect. A world where people like Saadia, who help others, are celebrated and thanked, not put in jail. Let's remember Saadia Mosbah and her big, kind heart. And let's promise to always try our best to be kind, fair, and welcoming to everyone, everywhere. Because when we are kind, we make the world a better, brighter place for all of us. Thank you for listening, super friends!
summary: A kind woman named Saadia Mosbah from Tunisia, who helped new people coming to her country feel welcome, has been put in jail for eight years because some powerful grown-ups disagreed with her helping.
whyMatters: This story teaches us that being kind and helping everyone, especially new friends, is very important, and we should always try to stand up for what is right and fair, even when it's hard.
